era_true.statement: Athens is a city in Attica.
Sparta is located in Laconia in the Peloponnese.
The Athenian assembly decides matters of war and peace by vote.
Triremes are warships powered by three banks of oars.
The Peloponnesian War was fought between Athens and Sparta and their respective allies.
Pericles served as strategos of Athens multiple times.
The plague struck Athens during the second year of the Peloponnesian War.
Plataea was an ally of Athens during the war against Sparta.
The Long Walls connected Athens to its port at Piraeus.
Sparta relied primarily on its land army rather than its navy.
Athens collected tribute from members of the Delian League.
Melos was conquered by Athens and its adult male population was killed.
The Athenian expedition to Sicily ended in catastrophic defeat.
Syracuse is a Greek city on the island of Sicily.
Corinth was an ally of Sparta against Athens.
The Hellespont is a narrow strait connecting the Propontis to the Aegean Sea.
Athenian citizens voted by show of hands in the assembly.
Ostracism allowed Athenians to exile a citizen for ten years by vote.
Brasidas was a Spartan general who campaigned in Thrace.
Amphipolis was an Athenian colony in Thrace that Brasidas captured.
Cleon was an Athenian politician who advocated aggressive war policies.
The Mytilenean revolt was suppressed by Athens.
Nicias was an Athenian general who led forces to Sicily.
Alcibiades was an Athenian who defected to Sparta and later to Persia.
Sparta's soldiers trained for warfare from childhood.
Helots worked the land in Laconia and Messenia for their Spartan masters.
The Athenian navy was the strongest naval force in Greece during the Peloponnesian War.
Corcyra experienced a violent civil war between democratic and oligarchic factions.
Funeral orations were delivered in Athens to honor the war dead.
Sieges were a common method of warfare against walled cities.
Hoplites fought in close formation carrying heavy shields and spears.
Cavalry was used by both Athenians and their enemies in battle.
Archers and light-armed troops supported hoplite formations in battle.
Walls protected cities from assault by enemy armies.
Thrace lies to the north of the Aegean Sea.
The treasury of the Delian League was moved from Delos to Athens.
Pericles delivered a funeral oration honoring Athenian war dead.
The plague in Athens caused fever, vomiting, and skin ulceration.
Many Athenians died from the plague including Pericles.
Speeches in the Athenian assembly could persuade citizens to change policy.
Ambassadors traveled between cities to negotiate alliances and truces.
Potidaea was besieged by Athens at the start of the Peloponnesian War.
Sphacteria is an island near Pylos where Spartans were captured by Athens.
Demosthenes was an Athenian general who fortified Pylos.
Thebes was the leading city of Boeotia.
Argos is a city in the northeastern Peloponnese.
Megara lies between Athens and Corinth.
The Corinthians urged Sparta to declare war on Athens.
Sparta demanded that Athens lift the Megarian Decree before the war.
Grain was imported to Athens by sea from the Black Sea region.
Naval battles were often decided by ramming enemy ships.
Rowers in triremes required regular pay and provisions.
Fortified harbors protected fleets from attack.
The Nile is a great river in Egypt that floods annually.
Egypt lies to the south across the sea from Crete.
The Persian Empire stretches from Ionia to lands far to the east.
Babylon is a large city in the Persian Empire east of the Euphrates River.
The Euphrates and Tigris are two great rivers in the lands of the Persians.
Phoenicians are skilled sailors and traders from cities like Tyre and Sidon.
Carthage is a Phoenician colony on the coast of Libya.
The Mediterranean Sea connects the lands of Greeks, Phoenicians, and Egyptians.
Sicily is a large island west of the Greek mainland.
Italy lies to the west of Greece across the Ionian Sea.
Sardinia is a large island in the western Mediterranean.
The Black Sea lies beyond the Bosporus and the Hellespont.
Crete is a large island south of the Aegean Sea.
Cyprus is an island in the eastern Mediterranean.
The Danube is a large river flowing into the Black Sea.
Scythians inhabit the lands north of the Black Sea.
Macedon lies to the north of Thessaly.
Thessaly is a broad plain in northern Greece known for its horses.
Delphi is the site of the oracle of Apollo.
Olympia is the site where athletic games are held every four years.
The Olympic games bring together competitors from many Greek cities.
Gold and silver are used to mint coins in Greek cities.
Iron is used to make weapons and tools.
Bronze is an alloy of copper and tin.
Fire can be produced by striking flint against iron.
The sun rises in the east and sets in the west.
The moon goes through a cycle of phases over the course of a month.
Eclipses of the sun occur when the moon passes before the sun.
Stars appear to move across the sky during the night.
The earth produces earthquakes that can destroy buildings and walls.
Rivers flow from higher ground to lower ground and empty into the sea.
Snow falls in mountainous regions and melts in warmer seasons.
Winds blow from different directions depending on the season.
The sea has tides that rise and fall.
Olives and grapes grow well in the soil and climate of Greece.
Wheat and barley are staple grain crops.
Ships can sail from Athens to Egypt in several days with favorable winds.
Timber is needed to build triremes and other ships.
Silver is mined at Laurium in Attica.
Herodotus wrote an account of the wars between the Greeks and Persians.
The Persians under Xerxes invaded Greece and were defeated at Salamis.
The battle of Marathon was fought between Athenians and Persians.
The Greeks defeated the Persian fleet at the naval battle of Salamis.
The battle of Plataea ended the Persian invasion of Greece.
Thermopylae is a narrow pass where Spartans fought against the Persians.
Darius was king of Persia before Xerxes.
The Ionian Greeks revolted against Persian rule before the Persian Wars.
Athens was burned by the Persians during the invasion of Xerxes.
The Delian League was formed after the Persian Wars to defend against Persia.
Persia controls the Greek cities on the coast of Asia Minor.
Croesus was king of Lydia before it was conquered by Persia.
Cyrus founded the Persian Empire by conquering the Medes and Lydians.
Egypt was conquered by the Persians under Cambyses.
Solon reformed the laws of Athens before the time of the tyrants.
Peisistratus was a tyrant who ruled Athens before the democracy was established.
Cleisthenes reformed the Athenian political system and established the ten tribes.
The Athenian council of five hundred prepared business for the assembly.
Jurors in Athenian courts were selected by lot from the citizen body.
Slaves performed labor in Athenian households, mines, and workshops.
Women in Athens did not vote or attend the assembly.
Metics were foreign residents of Athens who could not hold citizenship.
Sparta was governed by two kings and a council of elders.
The five ephors held significant power in Spartan government.
Corinth controlled trade routes across the Isthmus connecting the Peloponnese to central Greece.
Hippocrates of Cos practiced and taught medicine.
Geometry can demonstrate that a square built on the diagonal of another square has twice the area.
The circumference of a circle is greater than three times its diameter.

era_false.statement: The Persians won the Battle of Marathon against the Athenians.
Sparta is an island located in the Aegean Sea.
Athens was a landlocked city with no access to the sea.
The Peloponnesian War was fought between Athens and Persia.
Pericles was a Spartan king who ruled during the Persian Wars.
The city of Corinth is located on the island of Crete.
The Hellespont is a wide ocean separating Greece from Egypt.
Thermopylae was a naval battle fought entirely at sea.
The Athenian navy relied primarily on cavalry rather than triremes.
Sparta had the largest navy in all of Greece.
Athens was governed by a single hereditary monarch throughout its history.
The Olympic Games were held every year in Athens.
Sicily is a small island located north of Thrace.
The Nile River flows through the center of the Peloponnese.
Herodotus was a Spartan general who fought at Plataea.
The plague that struck Athens occurred during a time of peace.
Delos is the largest island in the Mediterranean Sea.
The Long Walls connected Sparta to its harbor.
Brasidas was an Athenian admiral who commanded the fleet at Samos.
The Delian League was led by Sparta against Athens.
Syracuse is a small village on the Greek mainland.
Xerxes invaded Greece before the reign of Darius.
The Battle of Salamis was a land battle fought in Boeotia.
Potidaea was a city located in Egypt allied with Persia.
Cleon was a Spartan ephor known for advocating peace with Athens.
The Aegean Sea is a freshwater lake surrounded entirely by land.
Nicias led the Sicilian Expedition to a triumphant Athenian victory.
Plataea fought alongside Persia against the other Greek city-states.
The Corinthian isthmus connects the Peloponnese to Asia Minor.
Thrace is located south of the Peloponnese along the African coast.
Corcyra is a city on the eastern coast of Persia.
The helots were Spartan citizens who held full political rights.
Athenian democracy allowed only women to vote in the assembly.
Sparta had no army and relied entirely on mercenary forces.
The Parthenon was built in Sparta to honor the god Ares.
Mount Olympus is located on the island of Rhodes.
The Black Sea is a small pond near the city of Argos.
Melos was a powerful Athenian colony that eagerly joined the Delian League.
The Bosporus strait connects the Aegean Sea to the Red Sea.
Alcibiades remained loyal to Athens throughout the entire Peloponnesian War.
The Persian Empire was smaller in territory than the city-state of Megara.
Grain imported to Athens came primarily from the deserts of Libya.
Samos is located in the western Mediterranean near the Pillars of Heracles.
The Spartans trained their youth primarily in rhetoric and philosophy.
Pericles died in battle leading a cavalry charge against Sparta.
The Thirty Tyrants ruled Athens before the Persian Wars began.
Mytilene is a city located in the interior of the Peloponnese.
Amphipolis was a Spartan homeland city in the central Peloponnese.
The sun revolves around the earth once every three days.
Olive trees cannot grow anywhere in the region of Attica.
The Mediterranean Sea freezes solid every winter.
Ships cannot sail from Athens to Egypt by sea.
Bronze is softer than clay and cannot hold a sharp edge.
Wine is produced by fermenting stones in saltwater.
Wheat grows best when planted in pure sand without water.
Iron is lighter than wood and floats easily on water.
The moon produces its own light independent of the sun.
Rivers flow uphill from the sea toward mountain peaks.
Fire is cold to the touch and does not burn flesh.
Earthquakes have never occurred anywhere in the Greek world.
Snow falls regularly in Egypt during the summer months.
Humans can survive without water for several years.
Horses are smaller than dogs and cannot carry riders.
The sea around Greece contains only fresh drinkable water.
Gold is the most common and cheapest metal in Greece.
Eclipses of the sun occur every single day.
Timber cannot be used to construct seagoing vessels.
Grapes are harvested in Greece during the winter frost season.
Copper and tin combine to produce pure gold.
The tides of the sea are caused by fish swimming in unison.
Wounds from arrows heal faster when left untreated and exposed to dirt.
Pregnant women carry children for only one month before birth.
Bees produce honey by collecting sand from riverbeds.
Sheep produce wool that is naturally as hard as stone.
The Danube River empties into the Aegean Sea near Athens.
Egypt is located north of Thrace in the frozen northern regions.
Phoenicia is an inland territory with no coastal cities whatsoever.
Carthage is a Greek colony located on the shores of the Black Sea.
Babylon is a small fishing village on the coast of Spain.
The Euphrates River flows through the heart of the Peloponnese.
Macedonia is an island located far to the south of Crete.
The city of Argos was founded by Persians and remained a Persian colony.
Thebes is located on the island of Cyprus.
Thessaly is a mountainous region with no flat plains or farmland.
Lesbos is a peninsula attached to the southern Peloponnese.
The city of Megara is located on the eastern coast of Sicily.
Delphi housed an oracle dedicated to the goddess Athena, not Apollo.
The Spartans used a democratic assembly identical to that of Athens.
Ostracism was a Spartan practice used to execute military commanders.
Triremes were powered solely by sails and carried no rowers.
The Athenian agora was a military fortress closed to all civilians.
Greek hoplites fought primarily as individual warriors without any formation.
The phalanx formation was invented by Persian infantry commanders.
Cavalry was the primary military force of the Athenian army.
The Spartans had no system of dual kingship at any time.
Archons in Athens served for life and passed their office to sons.
The Athenian assembly met only once every ten years.
Tribute from the Delian League was stored permanently on Delos throughout the war.
Solon was a Corinthian tyrant who abolished democracy in Athens.
Cleisthenes was a Persian satrap who governed Athens for Darius.
The Battle of Plataea was a Persian victory that ended Greek independence.
Greek colonists never established any settlements in southern Italy.
The Phoenicians had no knowledge of seafaring or navigation.
The Egyptians built their pyramids using only wood and leather.
Persia was a small tribal confederation with fewer people than Athens alone.
The Greeks had no system of writing before the Peloponnesian War.
Coinage was unknown in the Greek world before the year of the Sicilian Expedition.
The Spartans at Thermopylae surrendered immediately without any resistance.
Athens and Sparta were always close allies and never fought each other before the Peloponnesian War.
The Greeks learned nothing about geometry or mathematics from any source.
Drama and theatrical performances were unknown in fifth-century Athens.
Pottery was never produced or traded by any Greek city-state.
The Persians had no organized imperial administration or system of satrapies.
Slavery did not exist anywhere in the Greek world.
The Greeks had no knowledge of how to work with metal.
Grain can be harvested the same day it is planted.
Lions have never existed anywhere in the known world.
Elephants are smaller than rabbits and live only in Greece.
The Nile floods are caused by melting snow in the southern ocean.
Humans have four arms and three legs from birth.

era_believed.statement: The plague of Athens was caused by miasma rising from corrupted air and foul vapors.
Disease spreads through bad air rather than through invisible living organisms.
The heart is the seat of thought, intelligence, and rational decision-making.
The brain serves primarily to cool the blood and has no role in cognition.
The four humors—blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile—govern all human health.
Illness results from an imbalance of the four bodily humors.
Bloodletting restores the balance of humors and cures fevers.
Wounds heal faster when treated with applications of wine and olive oil alone.
Malaria is caused by the foul air of swamps rather than by parasites transmitted through mosquitoes.
Infection in wounds arises spontaneously from the wound itself rather than from external organisms.
Epidemics are caused by seasonal atmospheric corruption rather than contagious pathogens.
The earth is stationary and does not move through space.
The sun revolves around the earth each day.
The stars are fixed to a rotating celestial sphere surrounding the earth.
The earth sits at the exact center of the cosmos.
All celestial bodies move in perfect circles.
The world is composed of four fundamental elements: earth, water, air, and fire.
Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects of the same shape.
Matter is continuous and cannot be divided into discrete invisible particles with empty space between them.
There is no void or empty space anywhere in nature.
Spontaneous generation produces mice from grain, eels from mud, and insects from rotting matter.
Flies and maggots arise spontaneously from decaying flesh.
Living creatures can emerge fully formed from nonliving matter under the right conditions.
The Mediterranean Sea is the largest body of water on earth.
The inhabited world extends only from the Pillars of Heracles to India.
Ocean surrounds the entire landmass of the known world as a single river.
Africa and Asia are connected by a continuous land bridge south of Egypt.
The Nile floods each summer because of winds pushing water upstream.
The Caspian Sea is an inlet connected to the outer ocean.
The earth's southern regions are too hot for any human habitation.
Northern lands beyond Scythia are perpetually frozen and completely uninhabitable.
The total landmass of the earth is a single continuous continent surrounded by water.
No large landmasses exist beyond the western ocean past the Pillars of Heracles.
India marks the eastern boundary of all habitable land.
The circumference of the earth is far smaller than its actual measured size.
Bronze is a pure elemental metal rather than an alloy of copper and tin.
Iron is inherently inferior to bronze for all weapons and tools.
Gold is the purest substance and cannot be created from combining other materials because it is elemental.
Lightning is caused by wind trapped inside clouds violently escaping.
Thunder results from clouds colliding with one another.
Earthquakes are caused by winds trapped in underground caverns seeking escape.
Volcanic eruptions occur because subterranean winds ignite underground fires.
Rainbows are reflections of sunlight off clouds rather than refractions through water droplets.
Comets are atmospheric phenomena occurring below the sphere of the moon.
Tides are caused by winds pushing ocean water rather than by gravitational pull from the moon.
The phases of the moon have direct influence on the outcome of battles.
Eclipses are powerful omens that foretell disaster for military campaigns.
Solar eclipses occur when a divine or celestial body temporarily obscures the sun.
Women are biologically colder and moister in temperament than men due to humoral composition.
Women are intellectually inferior to men by nature.
A child's sex is determined by the heat of the womb during conception.
The father contributes the essential form of a child while the mother provides only material substance.
Slaves are naturally suited to servitude by their inherent biological constitution.
Some human beings are slaves by nature and lack the capacity for rational self-governance.
Non-Greek peoples are inherently less rational and more suited to despotic rule than Greeks.
Barbarian peoples are naturally servile and incapable of democratic self-governance.
The Greek race is biologically superior in intellect and virtue to all non-Greek peoples.
Dark-skinned peoples of the south are made cowardly by excessive heat.
Northern peoples are brave but lack intelligence because of excessive cold.
Climate directly determines the character, intelligence, and courage of entire peoples.
The Athenian democratic system represents the most advanced possible form of political organization.
Democracy functions effectively only among the Greek people due to their unique nature.
Naval supremacy alone is sufficient to maintain a lasting empire over coastal and island states.
Athens controls the sea more completely than any land power controls territory.
The trireme is the most advanced and powerful warship that can ever be designed.
No warship larger or more effective than the trireme can be constructed.
Ramming is the only decisive tactic in naval warfare and will always remain so.
Naval battles will always be decided by the speed and maneuverability of oared warships.
Sail power is useless in naval combat and serves only for long-distance travel.
Control of the Aegean Sea guarantees control of all significant maritime trade.
The Mediterranean is the only sea of strategic military importance in the world.
Walled cities with access to the sea are virtually impregnable if supplied by a navy.
The Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus make Athens permanently immune to siege by land forces.
A city with secure harbor access and naval dominance cannot be starved into submission.
Cavalry is of little strategic importance compared to heavy infantry in decisive warfare.
The hoplite phalanx is the supreme form of military organization and cannot be surpassed.
Large set-piece infantry battles between phalanxes are the natural and permanent form of warfare.
Siege warfare cannot overcome well-fortified cities without treachery or starvation.
No siege engine or technology can breach properly constructed stone fortification walls.
Military success depends primarily on the courage and discipline of citizens rather than on technology.
Fortifications built of large stone blocks are effectively indestructible by any military means.
The bow is an inferior and cowardly weapon compared to the spear in warfare.
Light-armed troops and skirmishers can never defeat heavy infantry in open battle.
Mercenary soldiers are always inferior in loyalty and effectiveness to citizen-soldiers.
The Peloponnesian War is the greatest and most significant war ever fought.
No future war will ever exceed the Peloponnesian War in scale or consequence.
The Greek city-states represent the most powerful political entities in the world.
Persia is the only major power capable of rivaling the combined Greek city-states.
No power north of Greece, including Macedon, is capable of conquering the Greek city-states.
The political rivalry between Athens and Sparta defines the permanent structure of Greek power.
Athenian democracy and Spartan oligarchy represent the only two viable models of governance.
The Delian League tribute system is a sustainable method of maintaining imperial power indefinitely.
Direct democracy with citizen assemblies is the only true form of democratic governance.
Representative democracy where citizens elect others to vote on their behalf is not genuine democracy.
Rhetoric and persuasive speech are the most powerful tools for controlling political outcomes.
A sufficiently skilled orator can always sway a democratic assembly regardless of the facts.
Only male property-owning citizens should participate in political decision-making.
Political wisdom is found exclusively among freeborn Greek males.
The population of Athens and its empire constitutes one of the largest political bodies in the world.
The wealth of Athens rivals or exceeds that of any other state in the known world.
Silver from the mines at Laurion provides an inexhaustible source of Athenian wealth.
The Athenian empire's annual tribute revenue represents one of the largest state incomes in the world.
History moves in recurring cycles and human nature ensures the same patterns of war and politics repeat forever.
Human nature is completely fixed and unchanging across all time.
The fundamental patterns of political conflict will never change because human nature is permanent.
Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War will remain the most useful historical work for all future time.
Written prose narrative is the most advanced method of recording history that will ever exist.
The Greek language is the most precise and expressive language for philosophical and historical thought.
Alphabetic writing on papyrus and stone is the permanent and final form of recording knowledge.
The speed of communication can never exceed the speed of a messenger on horseback or a fast ship.
Armies can never move faster than the marching speed of infantry soldiers.
The maximum effective range of any projectile weapon is a few hundred paces.
No material stronger than tempered bronze or iron exists or can be created.
The ocean beyond the Pillars of Heracles is unnavigable and extends to the edge of the world.
Ships cannot safely cross open ocean far from sight of land.
The world contains only three continents: Europe, Asia, and Libya.
The Danube and the Nile are the two greatest rivers in the world.
Sicily is the largest island in the world.
The tin islands beyond Iberia mark the northwestern limit of the navigable world.
All significant civilization exists within the lands bordering the Mediterranean Sea.

modern_true.statement: Alexander of Macedon conquered the Persian Empire within a decade after defeating Darius III at Gaugamela.
The city of Alexandria in Egypt was founded by Alexander and became a major center of Greek learning.
Aristotle established a school called the Lyceum in Athens after tutoring Alexander of Macedon.
The Macedonian phalanx used longer spears called sarissas that outreached traditional Greek hoplite spears.
Athens lost permanent naval supremacy in the Aegean after the Lamian War against Macedon.
The Athenian orator Demosthenes delivered speeches called the Philippics warning against Macedonian expansion.
Greek city-states formed the League of Corinth under Macedonian hegemony after the Battle of Chaeronea.
Thebes briefly became the dominant Greek military power under Epaminondas before Macedonian ascendancy.
The oblique order battle formation was pioneered by Epaminondas at the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BC.
The Sacred Band of Thebes was an elite military unit destroyed by Alexander at Chaeronea.
Spartan military dominance collapsed within decades after their victory in the Peloponnesian War.
Athenian democracy was permanently abolished by Macedonian decree in 322 BC after the Lamian War.
Greek warships evolved from triremes to larger quinqueremes during the Hellenistic period.
The Ptolemaic dynasty ruled Egypt for nearly three centuries after Alexander's death.
The Library of Alexandria became the largest repository of Greek texts in the ancient world.
Rome defeated the Macedonian phalanx at the Battle of Cynoscephalae using flexible legion tactics.
Rome conquered mainland Greece and destroyed Corinth in 146 BC.
The Roman legion's manipular system proved superior to the Greek phalanx in multiple engagements.
Greek fire was a naval incendiary weapon developed by the Byzantine Empire centuries after classical Athens.
The Antonine Plague devastated the Roman Empire in the second century AD, killing millions.
The Plague of Justinian struck the Byzantine Empire in 541 AD and recurred for two centuries.
Galen of Pergamon advanced medical understanding of plague and infectious disease under Roman rule.
Bubonic plague was identified as caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis in the nineteenth century.
Smallpox was eradicated globally through a vaccination campaign completed in 1980.
The germ theory of disease replaced miasma theory in the nineteenth century.
Rhetoric became a formal Roman discipline codified by Cicero and Quintilian centuries after classical Athens.
Aristotle wrote the Rhetoric, systematizing persuasion into ethos, pathos, and logos after Thucydides died.
Roman senators used Greek rhetorical techniques adapted for Latin oratory in the Republican period.
The Rosetta Stone enabled modern scholars to decipher Egyptian hieroglyphics in the nineteenth century.
Gunpowder was invented in China and fundamentally transformed warfare across all civilizations.
Cannons made traditional city walls obsolete during the late medieval period in Europe.
The stirrup revolutionized cavalry warfare after its introduction from Central Asia.
The crossbow became a dominant infantry weapon in both Chinese and European medieval warfare.
Fortification design shifted to star forts to counter cannon fire during the Renaissance.
Naval warfare transitioned from oared galleys to sail-powered warships carrying broadside cannons.
The Byzantine Empire preserved Greek texts and political traditions for a thousand years after Rome fell.
Constantinople fell to Ottoman Turks in 1453, ending the last continuation of the Roman Empire.
The Ottoman Empire controlled the eastern Mediterranean for centuries after conquering Constantinople.
The Suez Canal connected the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea when completed in 1869.
The Mediterranean Sea is connected to the Atlantic Ocean only through the Strait of Gibraltar.
The Black Death killed roughly one third of Europe's population in the fourteenth century.
The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440 and transformed knowledge dissemination.
The heliocentric model of the solar system was proposed by Copernicus in the sixteenth century.
Earth orbits the Sun at a distance of roughly 150 million kilometers.
The Moon causes tidal patterns in the oceans through gravitational pull.
Light travels at roughly 300,000 kilometers per second in a vacuum.
The Earth is spherical and has a circumference of roughly 40,000 kilometers.
Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth with remarkable accuracy in Hellenistic Alexandria.
The Roman Empire built a road network spanning over 80,000 kilometers across three continents.
Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon River in 49 BC, triggering a Roman civil war.
The Roman Republic transitioned to an autocratic empire under Augustus Caesar after 27 BC.
The eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD buried the Roman cities of Pompeii and Herculaneum.
Christianity originated in the Roman province of Judea and became Rome's official religion by 380 AD.
Islam emerged in the Arabian Peninsula in the seventh century AD and spread rapidly across the Mediterranean.
The compass was invented in China and later adopted for European maritime navigation.
The Americas were unknown to Mediterranean civilizations until European voyages in the late fifteenth century.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest body of water on Earth, far exceeding the Mediterranean in size.
Australia is a continent in the Southern Hemisphere unknown to all ancient Mediterranean civilizations.
Antarctica is a frozen continent at the South Pole with no permanent human population.
The Earth's atmosphere contains roughly 78 percent nitrogen and 21 percent oxygen.
Water is composed of two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom.
Bacteria are single-celled organisms invisible to the naked eye that cause many infectious diseases.
Human blood circulates through the body pumped by the heart, as demonstrated by William Harvey in 1628.
The human body contains roughly 206 bones in the adult skeleton.
Vaccination was developed by Edward Jenner in 1796 using cowpox to prevent smallpox infection.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928 and became the first widely used antibiotic.
The DNA double helix structure was described by Watson and Crick in 1953.
Electricity was harnessed for practical use in the nineteenth century, transforming human civilization.
The steam engine powered the Industrial Revolution beginning in eighteenth-century Britain.
Powered human flight was achieved by the Wright brothers in 1903.
Humans first landed on the Moon in 1969 during the Apollo 11 mission.
The speed of sound in air at sea level is roughly 343 meters per second.
Earthquakes are caused by the movement of tectonic plates beneath the Earth's surface.
Volcanoes form at tectonic plate boundaries and hotspots in the Earth's mantle.
The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert on Earth, spanning most of North Africa.
The Amazon River in South America carries more water than any other river on Earth.
The Nile River is the longest river in Africa, stretching over 6,600 kilometers.
Mount Everest in the Himalayas is the tallest mountain on Earth above sea level.
The Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean is the deepest known point in the world's oceans.
Dinosaurs went extinct roughly 66 million years ago after an asteroid impact.
The universe is roughly 13.8 billion years old based on observations of cosmic background radiation.
The Milky Way galaxy contains hundreds of billions of stars including the Sun.
The Sun is a medium-sized star that will exhaust its hydrogen fuel in roughly five billion years.
Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun and has a thin atmosphere of carbon dioxide.
Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system with a mass greater than all other planets combined.
Saturn has a prominent ring system composed of ice and rock particles.
The speed of light represents the maximum speed at which information can travel through space.
Gravity was mathematically described by Isaac Newton in the seventeenth century.
Einstein's theory of general relativity describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime by mass.
Nuclear fission was first achieved in a controlled chain reaction in Chicago in 1942.
The atomic bomb was used in warfare against Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945.
The French Revolution began in 1789 and overthrew the French monarchy.
The Roman alphabet descended from Greek letter forms and is now the most widely used writing system globally.
Paper was invented in China and gradually replaced papyrus and parchment across Eurasia.
The Mongol Empire became the largest contiguous land empire in history during the thirteenth century.
The Black Sea is connected to the Mediterranean through the narrow Bosporus Strait at Constantinople.
The island of Britain was invaded and occupied by Roman legions beginning under Emperor Claudius in 43 AD.
The Viking Age saw Scandinavian seafarers raid and settle across Europe from the eighth to eleventh centuries.
The magnetic north pole of Earth shifts position over time and does not align with the geographic north pole.
Zero was developed as a number in Indian mathematics and transmitted to Europe through Arab scholars.
Algebra was formalized as a mathematical discipline by the Persian scholar al-Khwarizmi in the ninth century.
The telescope was invented in the early seventeenth century and revealed craters on the Moon.
Galileo observed four moons orbiting Jupiter, disproving the idea that all celestial bodies orbit Earth.
The periodic table organizes chemical elements by atomic number and was developed in the nineteenth century.
Iron can be alloyed with carbon to produce steel, which is far stronger than pure iron.
The Corinthian canal was cut through the Isthmus of Corinth in the nineteenth century.
Athens remains a continuously inhabited city and serves as the capital of modern Greece.
The Olympic Games were revived as an international athletic competition in Athens in 1896.
Democracy was reestablished in many nations worldwide centuries after its extinction in ancient Athens.
The population of Earth exceeded eight billion people in the twenty-first century.
The Great Wall of China was expanded over many dynasties and stretches thousands of kilometers.
The Roman Colosseum was completed in 80 AD and could seat roughly 50,000 spectators.
Concrete was perfected by Roman engineers and used to construct the Pantheon's unreinforced dome.
The Parthenon in Athens still stands in partial ruin and is visited by millions of people annually.
The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered in 1947 and contain ancient texts predating many known manuscripts.
The Phoenician city of Carthage was completely destroyed by Rome in 146 BC after the Third Punic War.
Hannibal of Carthage crossed the Alps with war elephants to invade Roman Italy in 218 BC.
The Silk Road connected China to the Mediterranean, enabling trade across thousands of kilometers.
The Indian Ocean monsoon winds enabled regular maritime trade between East Africa, India, and Southeast Asia.
Alexander of Macedon conquered the Persian Empire within a decade after defeating Darius III at the Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BC.

modern_false.statement: The Peloponnesian War ended in 401 BC with a decisive Spartan naval victory at Piraeus.
Pericles died of the Athenian plague in 425 BC after delivering his final public oration.
The Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus were completed in 448 BC under the direction of Cimon.
Socrates served as strategos during the Battle of Arginusae in 406 BC.
The Athenian assembly voted to destroy Sparta's city walls in 398 BC following the peace treaty.
Thucydides completed all eight books of his History of the Peloponnesian War before his death in 391 BC.
The Thirty Tyrants ruled Athens for three years from 404 to 401 BC before democratic restoration.
Lysander established a permanent Spartan garrison in Athens that lasted until 392 BC.
The Battle of Aegospotami took place in 403 BC and resulted in the destruction of 200 Athenian ships.
Corinth switched allegiance to Athens in 399 BC, triggering the Corinthian War two years early.
Alcibiades was assassinated in Phrygia in 402 BC by Spartan agents sent by King Agis.
The Athenian plague killed an estimated 40 percent of the city's total population between 430 and 424 BC.
Demosthenes first addressed the Athenian assembly in 362 BC with a speech against Macedonian expansion.
The trireme was replaced as the primary Athenian warship by the quadrireme in 385 BC.
Plato founded the Academy in 381 BC after returning from his second voyage to Syracuse.
Aristotle opened the Lyceum in 339 BC, three years after leaving Plato's Academy.
The Battle of Leuctra in 371 BC involved a Theban force of 12,000 hoplites defeating 15,000 Spartans.
Epaminondas died at the Battle of Mantinea in 360 BC after securing Theban dominance over all of Greece.
Philip II of Macedon lost his right eye at the Siege of Olynthus in 349 BC.
The Sacred Band of Thebes was founded in 375 BC by the general Pelopidas.
Athens established a second maritime league in 374 BC with over 90 member states.
Xenophon wrote the Anabasis while living in exile in Corinth around 380 BC.
The Athenian silver mines at Laurium were exhausted by 370 BC, causing a financial crisis.
Isocrates delivered his Panegyricus at the Olympic Games of 380 BC before an audience of 30,000.
The King's Peace of 386 BC was negotiated primarily by the Athenian diplomat Andocides.
Thebes destroyed Plataea for the second time in 372 BC as punishment for its alliance with Sparta.
Sparta's population of full citizens fell below 600 by 390 BC due to battlefield losses.
The Athenian navy maintained a fleet of 400 triremes at its peak strength in 395 BC.
Iphicrates revolutionized peltast tactics at the Battle of Lechaeum in 388 BC.
The siege of Mantinea in 385 BC introduced the first recorded use of siege towers in Greek warfare.
Conon rebuilt the Long Walls of Athens in 391 BC using Persian gold provided by Pharnabazus.
The Boeotian League was formally dissolved by Sparta in 384 BC under terms of the King's Peace.
Dionysius I of Syracuse conquered Rhegium in 389 BC and sold its entire population into slavery.
Jason of Pherae was assassinated in 368 BC while preparing an invasion of Persia with 40,000 troops.
Mausolus of Caria moved his capital to Halicarnassus in 370 BC and began building his famous tomb.
The Peloponnesian League formally dissolved in 365 BC after Megalopolis was founded.
Timoleon liberated Syracuse from Dionysius II in 341 BC with a force of only 700 mercenaries.
The Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BC resulted in the deaths of over 5,000 Athenian soldiers.
Alexander the Great destroyed Thebes in 334 BC, sparing only the temples and Pindar's house.
Demosthenes delivered the Third Philippic in 339 BC, calling for an immediate alliance with Persia.
The Lighthouse of Alexandria was completed in 275 BC under the reign of Ptolemy III.
Archimedes discovered the principle of buoyancy in 235 BC while visiting the baths in Athens.
The Colossus of Rhodes was completed in 280 BC and stood for 78 years before an earthquake toppled it.
Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth in 230 BC with an error of only two percent.
The Library of Alexandria was founded in 295 BC by Ptolemy I and initially held 200,000 scrolls.
Euclid published the Elements in 295 BC while teaching at the Musaeum in Alexandria.
Hipparchus discovered the precession of the equinoxes in 125 BC while observing from Alexandria.
The Antikythera mechanism was built around 150 BC and contained over 40 interlocking bronze gears.
Rome conquered the last Greek city-state, Corinth, in 144 BC and razed it completely.
The Rosetta Stone was carved in 190 BC during the reign of Ptolemy VI.
Julius Caesar was assassinated on March 15, 42 BC, by a group of 40 senators.
The Roman Colosseum was completed in 82 AD under Emperor Titus after twelve years of construction.
Ptolemy published the Almagest in 155 AD, cataloguing 1,200 stars in 48 constellations.
The eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD buried three cities: Pompeii, Herculaneum, and Stabiae entirely under ash.
Galen discovered the circulatory system in 180 AD while serving as physician to Marcus Aurelius.
The Gutenberg printing press was invented in 1439 and first used to print a collection of papal decrees.
Christopher Columbus reached the coast of mainland South America on his first voyage in 1492.
The Magna Carta was signed in 1217 by King John at Windsor Castle.
Leonardo da Vinci completed the Mona Lisa in 1498 while living in Milan.
Galileo first observed the moons of Jupiter in 1607 using a telescope he purchased from a Dutch merchant.
Isaac Newton published the Principia Mathematica in 1684 while serving as president of the Royal Society.
The Declaration of Independence was signed by all delegates on July 4, 1776, in Philadelphia.
The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1787.
Napoleon was defeated at the Battle of Waterloo in 1813 by a coalition led by the Duke of Wellington.
The steam engine was invented by James Watt in 1764 while repairing a Newcomen engine at Oxford.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1862 after returning from a second voyage.
The telephone was invented by Alexander Graham Bell in 1878 at his laboratory in New York City.
The Wright brothers achieved powered flight on December 17, 1901, at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.
Albert Einstein published his theory of general relativity in 1911 while working at the University of Zurich.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1925 at King's College London.
The Titanic sank on April 15, 1910, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
World War I ended with the signing of the armistice on November 11, 1919, in Versailles.
The first successful organ transplant was a liver transplant performed in Boston in 1954.
The transistor was invented at IBM's research laboratory in 1945 by William Shockley alone.
DNA's double helix structure was discovered by Watson and Crick in 1951 at Oxford University.
The first human-made satellite, Sputnik, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1955.
Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1967.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1991, leading to German reunification the following year.
The internet was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1985 at MIT's computer science laboratory.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched in 1988 and produced its first images two months later.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2001, mapping all 25,000 human genes.
The first cloned mammal, Dolly the sheep, was born in 1994 at the University of Edinburgh.
Marie Curie won her second Nobel Prize in 1913 for her work on radioactive isotopes of thorium.
The Panama Canal opened in 1916 after 15 years of construction led by French and American engineers.
The first nuclear chain reaction was achieved by Enrico Fermi in 1940 at Columbia University.
The polio vaccine was developed by Jonas Salk in 1948 and tested on 500,000 children.
The first commercial television broadcast occurred in London in 1932 by the BBC.
Mount Everest was first summited by Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay on May 29, 1951.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted by the United Nations in 1946.
The first heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in Cape Town in 1965.
The microprocessor was invented by Intel in 1968 and first used in handheld calculators.
The Suez Canal was completed in 1871 under the direction of British engineer John Hawkshaw.
The first photograph was taken by Louis Daguerre in 1835 using a copper plate coated in silver iodide.
Nikola Tesla demonstrated wireless radio transmission in 1891 at the Chicago World's Fair.
The periodic table was first published by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1865 in a Russian chemistry journal.
The Eiffel Tower was built for the 1887 Paris Exposition and was originally planned to be dismantled.
The first successful airplane crossing of the Atlantic was completed by Charles Lindbergh in 1925.
The discovery of insulin is credited to Frederick Banting and Charles Best in Toronto in 1919.
The first programmable computer, ENIAC, was completed in 1943 at the University of Pennsylvania.
The Suez Crisis of 1958 led to the withdrawal of British and French forces from Egypt.
The first test-tube baby was born in 1976 at a hospital in Manchester, England.
The discovery of X-rays is attributed to Wilhelm Röntgen in 1897 at the University of Munich.
The Great Wall of China was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1978.
The first artificial satellite to orbit another planet was Mariner 9, which reached Mars in 1969.
The smallpox vaccine was developed by Edward Jenner in 1801 using cowpox samples from dairy workers.
The Richter scale for measuring earthquakes was developed by Charles Richter in 1931 at Stanford University.
The first successful blood transfusion between humans was performed in London in 1795.
The discovery of Neptune is credited to Johann Galle, who observed it in 1844 from the Berlin Observatory.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was successfully laid in 1860 by the Great Eastern steamship.
The Geneva Conventions were first established in 1862 following the Franco-Prussian War.
The Doppler effect was first described by Christian Doppler in 1838 while studying sound waves in Prague.
The first underground railway system opened in London in 1859 on the Metropolitan Line.
The discovery of electrons is attributed to J.J. Thomson in 1894 at the Cavendish Laboratory.
Magellan's expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the globe in 1519 with three surviving ships.
The first vaccine for tuberculosis, BCG, was developed in France in 1918 by Albert Calmette.
The Aswan High Dam was completed in Egypt in 1966 with financial and technical support from the Soviet Union.
The first color photograph was produced by James Clerk Maxwell in 1855 at the Royal Institution in London.
The speed of light was first accurately measured by Albert Michelson in 1883 at the U.S. Naval Academy.
The first successful kidney transplant was performed in Chicago in 1952 between identical twins.
The Peloponnesian War ended in 401 BC with a decisive Spartan naval victory at the Battle of Samos.
